title: An Entire Ancient Greek Philosophical Treatise Burned by Mount Vesuvius Has Been Deciphered with XãRay and AI Technologies
description:
in History, Philosophy, Technology |   	June 30th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Most of our conôÙcepôÙtion of StoôÙicism, an ancient school of thoug
body:
in History, Philosophy, Technology |   	June 30th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Most of our conôÙcepôÙtion of StoôÙicism, an ancient school of thought much feaôÙtured here on Open CulôÙture, derives from the writôÙings of just three figôÙures: EpicteôÙtus, MarôÙcus AureôÙlius, and Seneca the Younger. But there were othôÙer StoôÙics, and despite their antiqôÙuiôÙty, we may yet learn more about them. Take ChrysipôÙpus of Soli, who was offiôÙcialôÙly known as the SecôÙond Founder of StoôÙicism due to his influôÙence on its spread throughôÙout the Greek and Roman world. What we know of his demandôÙing work, we know because of refôÙerôÙences writôÙten on scrolls inadôÙverôÙtentôÙly preôÙserved in a vilôÙla in HerôÙcuôÙlaôÙneum when nearôÙby Mount VesuôÙvius eruptôÙed in the year 79. To date, most of those ãHerôÙcuôÙlaôÙneum papyriã have been unreadôÙable, but soon, thanks to techôÙnoloôÙgies like Xãray microôÙtoôÙmogôÙraôÙphy and artiôÙfiôÙcial intelôÙliôÙgence, that may change.
In 2023, we postôÙed about the decodôÙing of the first word of one such scroll, an achieveôÙment made with the incenôÙtive of prizes offered by a conôÙtest called the VesuôÙvius ChalôÙlenge. Now, says its webôÙsite, ãwe have comôÙpleteôÙly virôÙtuôÙalôÙly unwrapped and read PHerc. 1667 ã the scroll the VesuôÙvius ChalôÙlenge comôÙmuôÙniôÙty knows as Scroll 4 ã withôÙout ever touchôÙing its pages.ã
What appears to be litôÙtle more than a big hunk of charôÙcoal,ô furôÙther damôÙaged by sevôÙerôÙal physôÙiôÙcal unrolling attempts in less techôÙnoôÙlogôÙiôÙcalôÙly advanced times, turns out to be ãa philoôÙsophôÙiôÙcal treaôÙtise on ethics, and the eviôÙdence points to a StoôÙic work: it turns on human nature, impulse, and the moral progress of human beings.ã The scrolôÙlãs last preôÙserved colôÙumn even drops the name of ArisôÙtocreôÙon, ãnephew and disôÙciôÙple of the great StoôÙic ChrysipôÙpus,ã sugôÙgestôÙing it dates to the secôÙond cenôÙtuôÙry BC.

These colôÙlabôÙoôÙraôÙtive efforts, both techôÙnoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal and intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal, have made PHerc. 1667 ãthe first HerôÙcuôÙlaôÙneum papyrus to be digôÙiôÙtalôÙly unrolled and read in full, end to end, and made availôÙable for susôÙtained scholôÙarôÙly study.ã But there are also othôÙer texts still being deciôÙphered, includôÙing PHerc. 139, which has been idenôÙtiôÙfied as ãPhilodeôÙmus, On Gods, Book 8 ã a treaôÙtise by the EpiôÙcureôÙan philosoôÙpher whose works fill so much of this library.ã In their day, StoôÙicism and EpiôÙcureôÙanism stood as simôÙiôÙlar but rival philosoôÙphies, and it seems that the ownôÙer of the so-called VilôÙla of the Papyri (posôÙsiôÙbly Julius CaeôÙsarãs father-in-law) had an interôÙest in both of them. Ancient StoôÙics and EpiôÙcureôÙans carôÙried on a liveôÙly debate about how to live, some of whose arguôÙments were writôÙten down. If the necôÙesôÙsary techôÙnoloôÙgies conôÙtinôÙue to advance, perôÙhaps weãll one day be able to read them all and pick that conôÙverôÙsaôÙtion up right where they left it off. Learn more about the decodôÙing of the papyrus here and here.
via SmithôÙsonôÙian MagôÙaôÙzine
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Researchers Use AI to Decode the First Word on an Ancient Scroll Burned by VesuôÙvius

title: Hannah Arendt on ãPersonal Responsibility Under Dictatorship:ã Better to Suffer Than Collaborate
description:
Image by Bernd Schwabe, via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
Whenô EichôÙmann in JerusalemãHanôÙnah Arendtãs book about Nazi offiôÙcer Adolf Eichmannãs trialãcam
body:
Image by Bernd Schwabe, via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
Whenô EichôÙmann in JerusalemãHanôÙnah Arendtãs book about Nazi offiôÙcer Adolf Eichmannãs trialãcame out in 1963, it conôÙtributed one of the most famous of post-war ideasô to the disôÙcourse, the ãbanalôÙiôÙty of evil.ã And the conôÙcept at first caused a critôÙiôÙcal furor. ãEnorôÙmous conôÙtroôÙverôÙsy cenôÙtered on what Arendt had writôÙten about the conôÙduct of the triôÙal, her depicôÙtion of EichôÙmann, and her disôÙcusôÙsion of the role of the JewôÙish CounôÙcils,ã writes Michael Ezra at DisôÙsent magôÙaôÙzine, ãEichôÙmann, she claimed, was not a ãmonôÙsterã; instead, she susôÙpectôÙed, he was a ãclown.ãã
Arendt blamed vicôÙtims who were forced to colôÙlabôÙoôÙrate, critôÙics charged, and made the Nazi offiôÙcerô seem ordiôÙnary and unreôÙmarkôÙable, relievôÙing him of the extreme moral weight of his responôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty. She answered these charges in an essay titled ãPerôÙsonôÙal ResponôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty Under DicôÙtaôÙtorôÙship,ã pubôÙlished in 1964. Here, she aims to clarôÙiôÙfy the quesôÙtion in her titleô by arguôÙing that ifô EichôÙmann were allowed to repôÙreôÙsent a monôÙstrous and inhuôÙman sysôÙtem, rather than shockôÙingôÙly ordiôÙnary human beings, his conôÙvicôÙtion would make him a scapeôÙgoat and let othôÙers off the hook. Instead,ô she believes that everyôÙone who worked for the regime, whatôÙevôÙer their motives, is comôÙplicôÙit and moralôÙly culôÙpaôÙble.
But although most peoôÙple are culôÙpaôÙble of great moral crimes, those who colôÙlabôÙoôÙratôÙed were not, in fact, crimôÙiôÙnals. On the conôÙtrary,ô they chose to folôÙlow the rules in a demonôÙstraôÙbly crimôÙiôÙnal regime. Itãs a nuance that becomes a stark moral chalôÙlenge. Arendt points out that everyôÙone who served the regimeô agreed to degrees of vioôÙlence when theyô had othôÙer options, even if thoseô might be fatal. QuotôÙing Mary McCarthy, she writes, ãIf someôÙbody points a gun at you and says, ãKill your friend or I will kill you,ã he is temptôÙing you, that is all.ã
While this cirôÙcumôÙstance may proôÙvide a ãlegal excuse,ã for killing, Arendt seeks to define a ãmoral issue,ãô a SocratôÙic prinôÙciôÙple she had ãtakôÙen for grantôÙedã that we all believed: ãIt is betôÙter to sufôÙfer than do wrong,ã even when doing wrong is the law. PeoôÙple like EichôÙmann were notô crimôÙiôÙnals and psyôÙchopaths, Arendt argued, but rule-folôÙlowôÙers proôÙtectôÙed by social privôÙiôÙlege. ãIt was preôÙciseôÙly the memôÙbers of respectable sociôÙety,ã she writes, ãwho had not been touched by the intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal and moral upheaval in the earôÙly stages of the Nazi periôÙod, who were the first to yield. They simôÙply exchanged one sysôÙtem of valôÙues against anothôÙer,ã withôÙout reflectôÙing on the moralôÙiôÙty of the entire new sysôÙtem.
Thoseô who refused, on the othôÙer hand, who even ãchose to die,ã rather than kill, did not haveô ãhighôÙly develôÙoped intelôÙliôÙgence or sophisôÙtiôÙcaôÙtion in moral matôÙters.ã But they wereô critôÙiôÙcal thinkersô pracôÙticôÙing what Socrates called a ãsilent diaôÙlogue between me and myself,ã and theyô refused to face a future where they would have to live with themôÙselves after comôÙmitôÙting or enabling atrocôÙiôÙties.ô We must rememôÙber, Arendt writes, that ãwhatôÙevôÙer else hapôÙpens, as long as we live we shall have to live togethôÙer with ourôÙselves.ã
Such refusals to parôÙticôÙiôÙpate might be small and priôÙvate and seemôÙingôÙly inefôÙfecôÙtuôÙal, but in large enough numôÙbers, they would matôÙter. ãAll govôÙernôÙments,ã Arendt writes, quotôÙing James MadiôÙson, ãrest on conôÙsent,ã rather than abject obeôÙdiôÙence. WithôÙout the conôÙsent of govôÙernôÙmentô and corôÙpoôÙrate employôÙees, the ãleaderãÎ would be helpôÙless.ã Arendt admits theô unlikeôÙly effecôÙtiveôÙness of active oppoôÙsiôÙtion to a one-parôÙty authorôÙiôÙtarôÙiôÙanô state. And yetô when peoôÙpleô feel most powôÙerôÙless, most under duress, she writes, an honôÙest ãadmisôÙsion of oneãs own impoôÙtenceã can give us ãa last remôÙnant of strengthã to refuse.
We have only for a moment to imagôÙine what would hapôÙpen to any of these forms of govôÙernôÙment if enough peoôÙple would act ãirreôÙsponôÙsiôÙblyã and refuse supôÙport, even withôÙout active resisôÙtance and rebelôÙlion, to see how effecôÙtive a weapon this could be. It is in fact one of the many variôÙaôÙtions of nonôÙviôÙoôÙlent action and resistanceãfor instance the powôÙer that is potenôÙtial in civôÙil disôÙobeôÙdiôÙence.
We have examôÙple after examôÙple of these kinds of refusals to parôÙticôÙiôÙpate in a murôÙderôÙousô sysôÙtem or furôÙther its aims. Arendt was aware these actions can come at great cost. The alterôÙnaôÙtives, she argues,ô may be far worse.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
HanôÙnah Arendt Explains How ProôÙpaôÙganôÙda Uses Lies to Erode All Truth & MoralôÙiôÙty: Insights from The OriôÙgins of TotalôÙiôÙtarôÙiôÙanôÙism
HanôÙnah Arendtãs OrigôÙiôÙnal ArtiôÙcles on ãthe BanalôÙiôÙty of Evilã in the New YorkôÙer Archive
HenôÙry David ThoreôÙau on When CivôÙil DisôÙobeôÙdiôÙence and ResisôÙtance Are JusôÙtiôÙfied (1849)
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.

title: When Bill Murray Unexpectedly Adapted a W. Somerset Maugham Novel: The Razorãs Edge (1984)
description:
in Film |   	June 29th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

In sumôÙmer of 1984, AmerôÙiôÙcan popôÙuôÙlar culôÙture was domôÙiôÙnatôÙed byô GhostôÙbusters, a block
body:
in Film |   	June 29th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

In sumôÙmer of 1984, AmerôÙiôÙcan popôÙuôÙlar culôÙture was domôÙiôÙnatôÙed byô GhostôÙbusters, a blockôÙbuster that comôÙbined sharp comôÙeôÙdy and specôÙtacôÙuôÙlar visuôÙal effects on a scale ã and in an unlikeôÙly harôÙmoôÙny ã movieôÙgoôÙers had nevôÙer seen before. Its great sucôÙcess advanced the careers of everyôÙone involved, not least that of Bill MurôÙray. HavôÙing already been an earôÙly (if not immeôÙdiôÙateôÙly beloved) SatôÙurôÙday Night Live cast memôÙber and givôÙen much-praised perôÙforôÙmances in comeôÙdies like CadôÙdyshack,ô Stripes, and TootôÙsie, he brought his famousôÙly detached senôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty to the role of the ghost-bustôÙing Dr. Peter Venkman and thereôÙby became the most in-demand comôÙic actor in HolôÙlyôÙwood. When, less than six months latôÙer, The Razorãs Edge opened with MurôÙray in the starôÙring role, fans bought tickôÙets in hopes of more laughs.


	

Itãs not as if they hadôÙnãt been warned. The Razorãs Edgeô was adaptôÙed from a novôÙel by W. SomôÙerôÙset MaughôÙam, a popôÙuôÙlar writer in his day, but hardôÙly a straightôÙforôÙward humorist. On the proôÙmoôÙtionôÙal cirôÙcuit, MurôÙray stressed that this was ãa seriôÙous movie,ã not a comôÙeôÙdy but a draôÙma. NevôÙerôÙtheôÙless, both critôÙics and audiôÙences at the time had trouôÙble acceptôÙing him in the role of LarôÙry DarôÙrell, a once-lightôÙheartôÙed young man who comes back from World War I overôÙwhelmed by the need to venôÙture back out into the world in search of the ultiôÙmate truths of exisôÙtence. MurôÙray was driôÙven to make the film (for which he took pay only as co-screenôÙwriter) out of the deep idenôÙtiôÙfiôÙcaôÙtion he felt with the charôÙacôÙter, which can only have intenôÙsiôÙfied the sting of its failôÙure.
That LarôÙry was a felôÙlow Chicagoan only explains part of the appeal. MurôÙrayãs thirôÙtiôÙeth birthôÙday, the birth of his first child, and the death of friends like Doug KenôÙney and John Belushi (whoãs indiôÙrectôÙly euloôÙgized in the film) had put him in a reflecôÙtive state of mind, while his growôÙing wealth and fame brought perôÙsonôÙal and psyôÙchoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal chalôÙlenges of their own. The prospect of exotôÙic locaôÙtion shoots in Paris and the Himalayas, whereô LarôÙryãs periôÙpatetôÙic seekôÙing takes him, may have sweetôÙened the deal. RevisôÙitôÙed today,ô the result has plenôÙty of memôÙoôÙrable moments, some of them posôÙsessed of genôÙuine beauôÙty and grandeur. Alas, the stoôÙry MaughôÙam tells in the novôÙel,ô rich with the subôÙtleties of memôÙoôÙry, perôÙcepôÙtion, and decepôÙtion, doesôÙnãt surôÙvive the HolôÙlyôÙwood tenôÙdenôÙcies toward over-comôÙpresôÙsion and litôÙerôÙal-mindôÙedôÙness.


	

It must be said that some of the blame lies with MurôÙray himôÙself, whose goofôÙball instincts clash against the nineôÙteen-twenôÙties setôÙting; as he latôÙer admitôÙted, he and direcôÙtor John Byrum were wrong to insist on a periôÙod piece. (Just imagôÙine the posôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙties of MurôÙray playôÙing a returned VietôÙnam vetôÙerôÙan instead.) RegardôÙless, he conôÙtinôÙued to folôÙlow his inner LarôÙry in the afterôÙmath, decampôÙing to Paris with his young famôÙiôÙly in order to live and learn far from the AmerôÙiôÙcan scene he knew. It was there that he encounôÙtered the teachôÙings of the mysôÙtic G. I. GurôÙdôÙjiôÙeff, whose influôÙence on MurôÙrayãs perôÙsona weãve preôÙviôÙousôÙly feaôÙtured here on Open CulôÙture. That marked anothôÙer step along the path of expeôÙriôÙence that would lead him to play wisôÙer, sadôÙder, yet nevôÙer entireôÙly unfunôÙny charôÙacôÙters in picôÙtures like Wes AnderôÙsonôÙãsô RushôÙmore and Sofia CopôÙpoôÙlaãsô Lost in TransôÙlaôÙtion ã and, in so doing, win draôÙmatôÙic respectabilôÙiôÙty after all.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
The PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy of Bill MurôÙray: The IntelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal FounôÙdaôÙtions of His Comedic PerôÙsona
The Zen of Bill MurôÙray: I Want to Be ãRealôÙly Here, RealôÙly in It, RealôÙly Alive in the Momentã
LisôÙten to Bill MurôÙray Lead a GuidôÙed MedôÙiôÙtaôÙtion on How It Feels to Be Bill MurôÙray
An AniôÙmatôÙed Bill MurôÙray on the AdvanôÙtages & DisôÙadôÙvanôÙtages of Fame
Bill MurôÙray, the StrugôÙgling New SNL Cast MemôÙber, ApolôÙoôÙgizes for Not Being FunôÙny (1977)

Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: How Japan Invented Daisugi, the Ancient Method of Growing Lumber Without Cutting Down Trees
description: Ask anyôÙone, of most any age and in most any sociôÙety, how we get wood, and youãll hear one answer: by cutôÙting down trees. Itãs thereôÙfore n
body:
Ask anyôÙone, of most any age and in most any sociôÙety, how we get wood, and youãll hear one answer: by cutôÙting down trees. Itãs thereôÙfore natôÙurôÙal that any method of lumôÙber proôÙducôÙtion that leaves trees standôÙing will get a lot of attenôÙtion. Such has been the case with daisuôÙgi, the 600-year-old JapanôÙese techôÙnique weãve preôÙviôÙousôÙly feaôÙtured here on Open CulôÙture. The Leaf of Life video above explains just what it involves: ãSpeôÙcialôÙly plantôÙed cedar trees are pruned heavôÙiôÙly. Think of it as a giant bonôÙsai.ã While these operôÙaôÙtions take place bienôÙniôÙalôÙly, ãharôÙvestôÙing takes 20 years, and old tree stock grows up to 100 shoots at a time,ã proôÙducôÙing a stronger and more flexôÙiôÙble wood to boot.
Such an unusuôÙal method of culôÙtiôÙvaôÙtion, you may imagôÙine, must have arisen in unusuôÙal cirôÙcumôÙstances. As the video explains, daisuôÙgi was origôÙiôÙnalôÙly inventôÙed in the westôÙern JapanôÙese region of KitayaôÙma, well south of the OsaôÙka-Kyoto-Nara conurôÙbaôÙtion.
WorkôÙing under a shortôÙage of seedlings and flat terôÙrain, the arborists of KitayaôÙma develôÙoped this method of forestôÙing that made it posôÙsiôÙble to ãreduce the numôÙber of planôÙtaôÙtions, make the harôÙvest cycle faster, and proôÙduce denser wood as well.ã More than a litôÙtle of the demand for it owed to the fourôÙteenth-cenôÙtuôÙry elite vogue for sukiya-zukuri, an eleôÙgant form of resôÙiôÙdenôÙtial archiôÙtecôÙture much expandôÙed from the traôÙdiôÙtionôÙal JapanôÙese tea house.


	

For a more nuts-and-bolts ã or rather, trunks-and-branchôÙes ã explaôÙnaôÙtion of how daisuôÙgi is done, have a look at the video just above from Roji GarôÙdenôÙing. You first need aô sugi tree, also known as aô CrypôÙtomeôÙria japonôÙiôÙca or JapanôÙese redôÙwood, whose fast growth makes it all work. When it reachôÙes six or sevôÙen meters, which takes about as many years, ãyou do someôÙthing WestôÙern garôÙdenôÙers would nevôÙer dream ofã: cut the trunk at the height of half a meter, prune back the remainôÙing branchôÙes, and culôÙtiôÙvate the buds that appear on the remainôÙing ãplatôÙform seedôÙer.ã ConôÙtinôÙue regôÙuôÙlarôÙly prunôÙing the series of ãperôÙfectôÙly verôÙtiôÙcalã new trunks into which they grow, evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly removôÙing everyôÙthing but the top 30 cenôÙtimeôÙters on each. WithôÙin a decade, youãll end up with a good source of wood, if you need it, but also an ãever-changôÙing, interôÙestôÙing stateôÙment treeã ã that, as a bonus, will also look like someôÙthing out of a GhiôÙbôÙli movie.
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!

title: DeviantArt Is an All-in-One Platform for the Creative Economy
description: Before the days of of Reddit, Facebook, and most other social networks, DeviantArt was fostering an online community dedicated to artists and art
body:
Before the days of of Reddit, Facebook, and most other social networks, DeviantArt was fostering an online community dedicated to artists and art lovers. Featuring a vast array of exciting and innovative projects across photography, painting, design, comics, and much more, the platform has spent the last 25 years connecting creators, collectors, and sellers working in every style and medium.
DeviantArt is now home to a diverse, active global community of more than 108 million members. Offering far beyond the traditional social networks, the platform enables users to discover art, build audiences, and grow their creative businessesãall within a single ecosystem. ãAt its heart, DeviantArt is an art community,ã says Chief Revenue Officer Liat Karpel Gurwicz. ãToday, DeviantArt is a complete creative home, where artists can publish work, grow their audience, and earn, while collectors and art lovers can discover, curate, and directly support the creators they care about. Every tool they need is all in one place, built around the community thatãs been here for 25 years.ãô 

DeviantArtãs dedicated creator economy offers artists everything they need to build long-term creative careers where their audience already exists. A holistic monetization ecosystem for creators and sellers, including features such as Shop, Studio (a centralized backend dashboard), Discounts, Analytics, and other tools. ãIn 2025 alone, our community made over $23 million in sales. To put that in perspective, creator sales in 2025 actually exceeded the previous five years combined!ã Karpel Gurwicz adds. 
Plus, DeviantArt doesnãt allow third-party advertisements. Instead, the platform focuses on promoting its creators and only makes money when artists succeed.  DeviantArt fully bets on its community, the way a true creative network should.

ãStaying true to what makes the platform special guides every decision we make,ã Karpel Gurwicz says. ãFor artists, we provide the tools and infrastructure to connect, grow, and earn. For art lovers, it means offering a home to discover, browse, and collect art, while enabling meaningful, direct support for creators they care about.ã
Grow your practice and make meaningful connections with a diverse group of artists by becoming part of DeviantArtãs creative community. Join today!

schema.headline: DeviantArt Is an All-in-One Platform for the Creative Economy

title: Mark Rothko's Color Fields Mirror the Elements in a 'Weather-to-Painting Experiment'
description: Mark Rothko is known for his ãcolor fieldã paintings, a genre that was coined in the 1950s to describe his work specifically, along with peers lik
body:
Mark Rothko is known for his ãcolor fieldã paintings, a genre that was coined in the 1950s to describe his work specifically, along with peers like Barnett Newman and Clyfford Still. These works are generally characterized by their total abstraction and emphasis on clearly delineated areas, or ãfields,ã of different hues. One might also think of Josef Albersã seminal series titled Homage to the Square, which delved into the virtually infinite relationships between colors.
For Rothko, canvases were often very large, measuring upwards of 10 feet. The works inside the Rothko Chapel in Houston, for example, are architectonic, commanding the viewerãs complete attention and inviting us to slow down and truly immerse ourselves in a contemplative space. Even on social media, a new generation is discovering the artistãs meditative works as an antidote to visual and information overload. His oeuvre also forms the basis of a project called Current Rothko by New York-based designer and creative director Joonas Virtanen.

On the elegantly simple website, type in any location in the world and the algorithm will select a Rothko painting representative of the weather there. Currently, the catalogue contains 89 paintings, each of which are tagged with a color register, brightness, temperature, and mood so that the search engine can match them to the conditions of specific places at any moment.
Virtanen has sourced many of the images from WikiArt and WikiMedia, plus fair-use image databases from major institutions like the National Gallery of Art, MoMA, Tate, and others. Rothkoãs work remains copyrighted, as artworks generally enter the public domain after 95 years. Access to high-quality imagery is therefore limited to what Virtanan describes as small ãcappedã previews that could never be reproduced at scale.
While each image is accompanied by the title of work, the designer acknowledges that the platform is a work in progress, and details such as dimensions and medium would enrich the experience. In the meantime, since I began this article, Chicago has transitioned from 77 degrees and clear with ãNo. 5/No. 22ã (1950) to a cloudy, humid 81 degrees with a chance of ãBlack on Maroon (Seagram V)ã (1958).
For another art historical twist on the weather, check out Virtanenãs Plein Air weather visualizer. And if youãre in New York City, you might also get a kick out of his subway status tool, Tangled Lines.

schema.headline: Mark Rothko's Color Fields Mirror the Elements in a 'Weather-to-Painting Experiment'

title: Scan More than 60 Million Stars in the Most Detailed Photo of the Milky Way Ever Taken
description: In March 2025, the Euclid mission led by the The European Space Agency (ESA) enabled scientists to capture the highest resolution image ever taken
body:
In March 2025, the Euclid mission led by the The European Space Agency (ESA) enabled scientists to capture the highest resolution image ever taken of the dense, glowing center of the Milky Way galaxy. An enormous swarm of stars forms a bulge at the heart of the spiral, and researchers continue to search amid these billions of gaseous orbs for exoplanets, or any planet thatãs located outside of our solar system.
ãThe galactic bulgeãthe central region of our galaxyãis a vast, tightly packed structure filled mainly with old, cooler stars, giving it its characteristic yellow colour,ã ESA says. The photograph, which is taken with visible light, allows scientists to pinpoint exoplanets and measure their mass by monitoring tiny changes in starlight over time in a process called microlensing.



ESAãs Euclid image captures more than 60 million stars, plus other phenomena such as nebulae, bright star clusters, and molecular clouds that appear like amorphous, dark splotches in front of the brighter areas. Incidentally, to the human eye, they serve as a visual guide or cue to the incredible depth of field the image has actually captured.
ãFor comparison, Euclidãs sharpness and sensitivity in visible light is similar to theô NASA/ESA Hubble Space Telescopeãsô wide field camera,ã ESA says. ãBut each pointing that Euclid captures in a few hours spans an area 270 times larger than Hubbleãs field of view. To observe the same Euclid mosaic, theô Keck Observatoryô would need around 2,000 hours.ã
Zoom in on the Galactic Bulge Survey image on ESASky.
A molecular cloud in front of a field of stars
A nebula in the Milky Wayãs galactic bulge
A star cluster

schema.headline: Scan More than 60 Million Stars in the Most Detailed Photo of the Milky Way Ever Taken
